name: Las Cruces
headline: Landscaping Services in Las Cruces, NM
intro: David has lived and worked in Las Cruces since 2007. Every yard he designs accounts for the alkaline caliche soil, the July-through-September monsoons, and summer days that regularly top 100°F. Whether it's a courtyard renovation near the Plaza or a full property install on the east mesa, each project starts with what the desert demands.
localContext: Because David is local, he can walk your property the same week you call. He knows the watering schedules from Las Cruces Utilities, the permit process through Community Development, and which plants actually make it through their first Mesilla Valley summer without constant attention.

neighborhoods.name: Sonoma Ranch
neighborhoods.description: Newer master-planned community with strict architectural review. David handles the HOA paperwork and designs within the approved guidelines.

neighborhoods.name: Picacho Hills
neighborhoods.description: Foothills properties with steep grades and panoramic valley views. Terraced walls, slope stabilization, and irrigation zoned for varied sun exposure.

neighborhoods.name: Mesilla Historic District
neighborhoods.description: Thick-walled adobes and courtyard gardens within the historic district boundary. Period-appropriate stone, shade-tolerant natives, and attention to design guidelines.

neighborhoods.name: University Hills / NMSU
neighborhoods.description: Established '60s–'80s homes where high water bills are the main complaint. Lawn removal, drip conversion, and low-water shade trees make the biggest difference here.

neighborhoods.name: Telshor / East Mesa
neighborhoods.description: Flat, exposed lots that take the full force of spring wind. Windbreak rows, secured decomposed granite, and tough native shrubs are the standard approach.

neighborhoods.name: Organ Mountain Foothills
neighborhoods.description: Acreage properties with rocky soil and BLM-adjacent land. Defensible-space plantings, monsoon grading, and retaining walls for steep terrain.

name: Mesilla
headline: Landscaping Services in Mesilla, NM
intro: Mesilla's thick-walled adobes, courtyard entries, and narrow streets need landscaping that respects the village — not a suburban template dropped on top of it. David works with the heavy river clay soil, the shade from mature pecans and cottonwoods, and the design guidelines that protect the historic district.
localContext: David has done everything from full courtyard redesigns around the Plaza to simple irrigation repairs on side streets off Calle de Santiago. He's familiar with the district review process and picks materials that fit the character of the area.

name: Mesilla Park
headline: Landscaping Services in Mesilla Park, NM
intro: Mesilla Park lots are some of the most spacious in the valley, which gives homeowners room for patios, shade trees, and real outdoor living spaces. The catch is that valley floor soil here holds water differently than the mesa, and older irrigation lines often need a full rework.
localContext: David handles projects throughout Mesilla Park regularly — everything from full-yard xeriscape conversions to targeted sprinkler repairs. Proximity to the shop means fast response and easy follow-up visits.

name: Doña Ana
headline: Landscaping Services in Doña Ana, NM
intro: Doña Ana is rural — larger lots on sandy soil, many running off private wells instead of city water. Rabbits and javelina will eat new plantings overnight if you don't plan for them, and the open terrain funnels spring wind straight across the properties. Landscaping out here has to account for all of that or it won't last.
localContext: David builds irrigation systems matched to private well output and mineral levels, picks species that browsing animals leave alone, and keeps scope realistic so a half-acre-plus property looks intentional without an oversized price tag.

name: Organ
tagline: Landscaping at the Base of the Organs
headline: Landscaping Services in Organ, NM
intro: Properties in Organ sit on rocky desert foothills where the terrain slopes, the soil is thin over bedrock, and monsoon runoff can move serious volume downhill. Most lots are large, many border BLM land, and the elevation brings slightly different growing conditions than the valley floor.
localContext: David has built retaining walls on steep Organ grades, installed defensible-space landscaping for properties near the wildland boundary, and designed drainage systems that handle the monsoon runoff these slopes produce.

name: Anthony
headline: Landscaping Services in Anthony, NM
intro: Anthony is the southernmost stop in Doña Ana County, right on the state line. The lower valley heat is extreme — even by local standards — and the soil ranges from heavy river clay near the Rio Grande to sandy hardpan on the east side of town. Older neighborhoods have irrigation systems that predate modern drip technology.
localContext: David works in Anthony on a regular basis. He matches plant selections and irrigation design to the extra heat exposure down here, where a setup that works fine in Las Cruces can struggle without adjustments.

name: Sunland Park
headline: Landscaping Services in Sunland Park, NM
intro: Sunland Park has grown fast with new residential construction along the state line. The terrain is rocky and exposed, wind sweeps off the mesa from the west, and afternoon sun hits hard on south-facing lots. New-build homes often come with minimal builder landscaping that needs a real upgrade.
localContext: David brings the same approach to Sunland Park that he uses across the valley — xeriscape, hardscape, and irrigation designed for the specific conditions of the property. Artificial turf has become especially popular here for homeowners who want a clean look without constant upkeep.

name: Picacho Hills
headline: Landscaping Services in Picacho Hills, NM
intro: Picacho Hills lots range from a third of an acre up to several acres on the western foothills, with significant grade changes and sweeping views across the Mesilla Valley. Every project here starts with slope management — retaining walls, erosion grading, and drainage routing — before any planting goes in.
localContext: David knows the Picacho Hills HOA rules and handles the architectural review paperwork. He's built multi-level retaining wall systems on these slopes and designs irrigation zones that account for the different sun exposure on each face of a hillside lot.

name: Sonoma Ranch
headline: Landscaping Services in Sonoma Ranch, NM
intro: Sonoma Ranch homes sit on the east mesa with shallow caliche under sandy topsoil and strong west-to-east wind exposure. The HOA requires front-yard xeriscaping and enforces approved plant lists, gravel colors, and wall height limits. Every design here has to pass architectural review before a shovel hits the ground.
localContext: David has done enough projects in Sonoma Ranch to know the review process cold. He drafts plans from the approved lists, submits the paperwork himself, and designs yards that satisfy the committee on the first pass.

name: East Mesa
headline: Landscaping Services in East Mesa, NM
intro: The East Mesa stretches from the Telshor commercial corridor out toward the Organ Mountain foothills. It's one of the windiest parts of Las Cruces — open, flat terrain with sandy soil over caliche hardpan. A lot of homes out here were handed off by the builder with minimal landscaping, and that original work rarely survives long.
localContext: David replaces underperforming builder-grade yards with windbreak plantings, secured gravel systems, and drip irrigation that doesn't get torn up by the gusts. Artificial turf has become a go-to option for East Mesa homeowners who want a clean yard without the constant maintenance battle.

name: Las Alturas
headline: Landscaping Services in Las Alturas, NM
intro: Las Alturas is a well-established neighborhood with homes dating from the 1980s and '90s. The yards here have mature trees, original hardscaping, and irrigation systems that are often 25+ years old. Most homeowners aren't starting from scratch — they need targeted upgrades that work with what's already there.
localContext: David assesses what's worth keeping and what needs to go. Sometimes it's a full xeriscape conversion; sometimes it's replacing a failing sprinkler system with drip and refreshing the rock beds. The goal is a smart renovation, not a demolition job.

name: Talavera
headline: Landscaping Services in Talavera, NM
intro: Talavera and the Soledad Canyon corridor sit northeast of town on foothill terrain with rocky soil, Organ Mountain views, and wide-open sun exposure. Lots are bigger out here, and the wind picks up speed crossing the open mesa before it hits the foothills.
localContext: David designs for Talavera's specific conditions — drought-tolerant species that anchor well in rocky ground, heavy gravel secured against wind, and drip systems zoned to keep water on the plants instead of blowing past them.

name: University Hills
headline: Landscaping Services in University Hills, NM
intro: University Hills is the neighborhood closest to NMSU — rows of 1960s and '70s ranch-style homes with big shade trees and front yards that haven't been updated in decades. The typical property has an old grass lawn running up the water bill, mature elms dropping limbs, and original copper or PVC irrigation that's well past its service life.
localContext: David works in this neighborhood constantly — ripping out turf, converting to drip, trimming overgrown canopies, and redesigning front yards that have been on autopilot for 30 years. He knows the root systems, the soil quirks near the arroyo, and the fastest path to a lower water bill.

name: Dripping Springs
headline: Landscaping Services in Dripping Springs, NM
intro: Dripping Springs properties are up in the foothills near the National Monument on large, rugged lots with natural desert vegetation, thin rocky soil, and real elevation changes across the property. This isn't suburban landscaping — it's working with the desert, not replacing it.
localContext: David takes a conservation-minded approach out here: defensible-space plantings near the home, drainage grading that routes monsoon water safely downslope, and hardscape materials that look like they belong on the mountain rather than a parking lot.

name: Fairacres
headline: Landscaping Services in Fairacres, NM
intro: Fairacres sits on the west side of Las Cruces, where pecan orchards and quarter-acre-to-three-acre lots give homeowners more room to work with than most in-town properties. The valley floor soil here is a different animal from the caliche mesa — heavier alluvial ground tied to the old irrigation-district canal network — and older systems built for farmland don't always translate cleanly to a modern residential yard.
localContext: David plans Fairacres properties around the acreage, not a standard suburban template — irrigation zoning has to account for real distance and pressure loss across a bigger lot, and valley soil holds water longer than caliche, which changes plant selection and drainage decisions. Whether it's a full redesign around existing pecan trees or a system sized to actually cover a few acres, he plans for the lot you have.
